text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A. Rahman
Riverside Health Centre

12 Medway Street
Southwark
London SE1 2AB
Dear Dr A. Rahman
Daniel Kerr, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H728591 NHS No. 9035172640
Flat 3, 21 Clapham Road, London SW9 0HX 

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification; prior EEGs inconclusive; MRI brain (2017) structurally unremarkable. Occupational setting includes prolonged standing and exposure to unpredictable crowd surges at a busy metro station gate line.

Previous medication: Levetiracetam (mood irritability), sodium valproate (weight gain, tremor), carbamazepine (dizziness), lamotrigine (rash), topiramate (word-finding difficulty), lacosamide (no clear benefit), clobazam PRN (sedation). Not previously trialled: perampanel, zonisamide, brivaracetam, pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide.

Comorbidities and relevant history: Migraine without aura; occasional orthostatic light-headedness; no known structural cardiac disease; vitamin D insufficiency (treated). No history of febrile convulsions. Family history non-contributory. 

I reviewed Daniel Kerr in clinic today, unaccompanied. Since our last contact in May, he reports that events have been sparse; specifically, he has not noted any further turns for several weeks, including during recent late shifts. He describes continuing prodromal sensations on two occasions (a brief wave of queasiness and metallic taste, each <30 seconds) without progression to a collapse or witnessed convulsion. He has maintained a seizure diary and cross-checked with workplace incident logs; there have been no duty interruptions due to episodes during this interval. He remains vigilant around the gate line during peak passenger flow and wears an ID band. 

Current medication:
- Brivaracetam 50 mg twice daily (initiated June; tolerated)
- Thiamine 100 mg daily
- Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily

Work and safety context: Role involves prolonged standing, exposure to heat during rush hours, and occasional rapid crowd movement. He has arranged, with his manager, access to a staff rest area for brief recovery if prodrome recurs. Hydration is encouraged on shift; he uses a small belt pouch water bottle. Manual handling of barrier gates is shared with colleagues during peak surges. 

Examination today: Alert, oriented; speech fluent; cranial nerves intact; no focal neurological deficits. Cardiovascular exam unremarkable. BMI stable. 

Investigations: No new imaging. Routine bloods in August were within normal limits (U&E, LFT, FBC). Brivaracetam level not routinely monitored. 

Plan:
- Continue brivaracetam 50 mg twice daily for now, given the recent period with essentially no breakthrough events. We discussed that should warning symptoms intensify or if collapses recur, we would consider dose optimisation or an alternative agent (e.g. zonisamide) after risk–benefit review.
- Reinforce workplace measures: avoid missed meals, maintain hydration, and ensure colleagues are aware of his agreed rest protocol during crowd surges. He will continue diary and incident log correlation.
- I have provided a personalised "what to do" card for colleagues regarding first aid for a possible seizure on the concourse (avoid restraint, clear space, time the episode, recovery position if needed). 
- DVLA guidance was reviewed today as per current standards; he confirms he is not driving. 
- Follow-up in 4 months, sooner if any deterioration or if he experiences a loss of awareness or collapse. He will contact the epilepsy nurse specialist line if prodromes become more frequent. 

Side effects and risks were reviewed, including somnolence and mood changes with brivaracetam. He has not noted aggression or mood lability since initiation. We discussed sleep hygiene and migraine triggers. 

Please let me know if there are any issues from your side. Thank you for your ongoing care.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he has not noted any further turns for several weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 02 Oct 2025. The letter states there have been no further seizure-like events ('turns') for several weeks, and only two brief prodromal sensations without seizure progression. There is no explicit numeric seizure rate over a defined period, and the seizure-free interval is less than 6 months, so the 'seizure free' labels do not apply. Because no quantifiable frequency (per day/week/month/year) is provided, the current seizure frequency cannot be determined.
seizure_frequency_number: he has not noted any further turns for several weeks

flaws.description: Gold (unknown) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
